--- SOURCE 1: Climate change costs Africa up to 5% of GDP, UN climate head says ---
URL: https://www.reuters.com/world/africa/climate-change-costs-africa-up-5-gdp-un-climate-head-says-2024-09-05/

SUMMARY:
Climate change costs Africa up to 5% of GDP , UN climate head says. NAIROBI, Sept 5 (Reuters) - The impact of global warming is costing African nations up to 5% of their economic output, the United Nations climate chief said on Thursday, calling for more investments to help adapt to climate change.

# Climate Change and Economic Development in Africa

### An Overview

Recent studies indicate that climate change will negatively impact Africa's growth and development prospects, particularly in the absence of adaptation and mitigation. Losses of up to 4% of Africa's gross domestic product (GDP) are projected, and agriculture is a major economic impact channel given the high share of Africa's GDP accounted for by rain-fed agriculture. This calls for concerted efforts by African policy-makers and development partners to help Africa build adaptive capacity to reduce vulnerability. Climate finance is a critical ingredient in the efforts to build Africa's adaptive capacity and also in mitigation. Climate funding pledges made by the developed countries have not been realised however, with a large variance between pledges and actual contributions. On the other hand, the uptake of available climate funding for Africa has been slow, in part due to week capacity of African countries to develop and execute adaptation measures. Another important contribution from the papers in this supplement is the treatment of uncertainty in the assessment of economic impacts of climate change. Two of the complicating factors in the assessment of economic impacts are (i) uncertainty associated with climate change and (ii) the fact that climate can be very localised, with different climates for proximate localities. A methodological approach for handling these two challenges is developed and applied to the country of Malawi. Results indicate that overall implications of climate change on GDP growth are relatively small to 2050, but the losses from climate change tend to increase over time, suggesting that impacts may become larger post 20150.

IMAGE 1:
URL: https://www.mdpi.com/sustainability/sustainability-15-07136/article_deploy/html/images/sustainability-15-07136-g001-550.jpg
DESCRIPTION: None

IMAGE 2:
URL: https://www.ipcc.ch/report/ar6/wg2/downloads/figures/IPCC_AR6_WGII_Figure_9_037.png
DESCRIPTION: The data presents observed changes in GDP per capita in various African countries due to climate change, projected economic impacts from a 4°C increase in global temperatures, benefits from limiting warming to 1.5°C versus 2°C, and the associated probabilities of economic gains from such limitations.

IMAGE 3:
URL: https://i1.wp.com/www.brookings.edu/wp-content/uploads/2019/10/20191018_ES_THP_ClimateFacts_Figure_3.jpg
DESCRIPTION: The map displays the projected change in per capita GDP by country in 2100 due to climate change, with regions color-coded to indicate varying degrees of economic impact, ranging from substantial losses (shown in red) to increases (shown in blue).

IMAGE 4:
URL: https://www.rbcgam.com/_assets/images/infographics/climate-change-in-emerging-markets-exhibit-03.jpg
DESCRIPTION: A world map illustrates the projected average real GDP loss by 2050 for different regions, indicating that Africa may experience the highest loss at 4.7%, while North America is expected to see a loss of 1.1%.

IMAGE 5:
URL: https://africacenter.org/wp-content/uploads/2021/10/Climate_GDP-01.png
DESCRIPTION: A graph illustrating the projected impact of climate change on Sub-Saharan Africa's GDP, showing increasingly negative percentages over time under various warming scenarios, with the most severe impacts under a "business as usual" model.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Projected GDP losses in Africa due to climate change range from 4% to 5%, significantly impacting economic growth and development.
North America is expected to experience a modest GDP loss of 1.1%, indicating regional variations in climate impact severity.

enriched_points.point_title: Southern U.S. coastal states at higher risk
enriched_points.expanded_content: Southern coastal states face heightened risks from climate change, including increased flooding and hurricanes, threatening local economies and livelihoods.
Long-term projections indicate worsening economic conditions as climate change progresses, necessitating urgent adaptation efforts to mitigate impacts.
Effective resilience planning is crucial for vulnerable regions to adapt to climate impacts, safeguard economies, and enhance community preparedness.
